item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Posting here due to the relevance of the topic.

As commercial use is prohibited, this is non-compliant with the site's CC 3.0 license and as such the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The dialogue is really well written.

Also poor 1073.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you know whats interesting is that the [data expunged] substance in SCP-1073-1 is most likely silicon tetrachloride, it is a liquid at STP, it is used to create high purity silicon for use in computers and it also reacts with water explaining what the the other [data expunged] substance it reacted with is (its water)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would upvote based just on staff behaving like rational researchers and not suppressing tech discoveries just because they were inspired by a skip.

But the science is solid enough for sci-fi, the writing is smooth, and I had a moment of genuine emotion for a pool of cave sludge.

This absolutely deserves its 100+ status.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's a better phrase to use? And why wouldn't a doctor use that phrase?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm having a really tough time believing that a doctor would say "based off", especially a doctor smart enough to be working for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to believe that this is the related to the church of the broken god, but alas it is not in the tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor little guys…
Just realized my first SCP actually hit +100. Woo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't know how/why it happened, but the spacing got screwed up. I've fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:/

This is a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of the interview logs show no text for the interviewer

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, it is catchy and the idea of it being inside all modern computers is very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got it. Thanks for pointing it out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: RAM is Random Access Memory, but this article lists it as Rapid Access Memory. Permission to fix this?

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The image in this article is a CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 image from Flickr. Note that the image is used here under a different license than the rest of the site. Commercial use of the image is not permited by its creator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
when a highly intelligent colony of living electronics (with emotions and unknown technological capabilities) wants to know more about world history and advanced mathematics the correct response is no.damn.way.

And we also double check their room was built inside a Faraday cage like we asked because we are professional paranoids and pessimists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically, what other people have been saying: The O5 approval to give these things whatever they want and the fact that real-world modern technology was reverse engineered from these things kill the article for me.

First of all, the "sure, go ahead" from the O5. This is specifically mentioned in one of the guides, I forget which one…I believe the exact phrasing was something along the lines of "The Foundation is a prison, not a hotel". The Foundation doesn't cater to the whims or desires of contained sapient entities just because - if they allow something, it's for a specific reason. If anything, "but it's nice and it wants it!" is a reason to not give something what it requests.

Second, modern technology being reverse-engineered from these little guys. Uh, the Foundation doesn't distribute advanced technological information to the general public - on the contrary, its job is to prevent such information from reaching the public. If the Foundation people don't understand how these creatures work, then the scientific benefits they provide need to be contained - and if they do understand it, it doesn't qualify as a scip. The Foundation contains a lot of useful things, like the fountain of youth and SCP-500, just because they can't explain them. I mean, I can see the argument "We understand the technology, just not how these things are sapient", but still, the point of the Foundation is to keep things where they are, not advance them. It's "secure, contain, protect", not "secure, protect, and use to better the world". Am I making sense? If not, could someone please help me out?

Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure how I feel about the whole "this is how we got computing technology" thing, but I really do like the rest of the article. The notes at the end feel a bit clumsy, however, although I'm not exactly sure what I'd suggest as a fix - I like the information being presented, I just think the way it's presented is a bit awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, although I don't like the bit in which our computing technology derives from them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this strangely endearing, and I don't know why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just don't know what to think of this. I like it a lot overall, but some of the writing (particularly dialogue) is just clumsy. Plus the 'lol they are responsible for real world computer stuff lol'… Ugh.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw. I feel sad for it (them?) now… =(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, pf, no, I wasn't actually offended or anything, just found it hysterical that it's that close to home XD Had a similar reaction to the "we own patents on a bunch of tech stuff" scene from MIB

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you really think the foundation would seed information without covering up their presence? ;)

Anyway, no disrespect is meant to your father or any of the other people who worked on the project. Next time you see him tell him he's awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Tempting fate" sums up that final note perfectly. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice! I'm glad my original concept was added upon, I really like what you've done with it, especially the emotional connection to the doctor shown by 1073-2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I was think of O5-3. Thank you for the story name, I've been looking around for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're referencing Ecce Perag and An Non, neither of the Sixes in those stories are a tech guy. You might want to read them again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Oh, neat, silicone life forms in an easily contained biosphere. That apparently work like living microchips.
…..
Wait we based the microchip off these things? Bell Labs!? *gets on the phone* Dad, we need to talk…"

Yes. My father was working at Bell Labs when they more-or-less designed the current setup we now call the PC. And yes I'm overly simplifying it, but anyway…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed, and backup plan added.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great! It's nice to see a friendly SCP, even if it isn't one we fully understand. The logs were touching, and I especially like the suggestion that we got current computer technology from reverse engineering these things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. The morse code is a neat touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this supposed to be the Brain Coral, or Com-Pewter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The O5-6 notes were a reference to tale about killing all the O5's (if I'm remembering correctly) O5-6 was the original tech support guy for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought I made that clear…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took a chance on making the object overly friendly because it's so alien. It's hard to claim a pile of sand stuck in a bucket of water can cross the "able line".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The notes kinda killed it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is much better than the old one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What Bright said. At least the Communist spiders had a proposed means to do that.

Also, I can hardly express the disservice the note does to it. I mean

Almost 8█% of the world's computer systems are based off information this thing gave us

Why the hell would you censor the second digit? Besides, why censor the number at all? To anyone who has any idea about how computers work, it's friggin' obvious over a half uses silicon -based transistors, and the exact percentage is a ) hard to obtain , b) doesn't bring in anything useful at all.
Besides, an O5 being complacent is very, very OOC.

Also ,very wrong history - IIRC transistor was invented in Bell Labs lots of time before Intel ever existed. Alternate historical interpretations have to seamlessly match with the real thing to be intriguing.

This is a pity because I almost upvoted while reading the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How the hell is it communicating with us? Especially in english? Sorry, but there's nothing in the description that allows me to suspend my disbelief over this bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Judging by the third test, I'd say that if it goes rogue, you introduce it to distilled water and lots of it. Really, there is absolutely no SCP that shouldn't have reasonable contingency plans, just in case, and it's certainly not the case than an O5 shouldn't be worried about it. I bet that, even though it's not mentioned in the article, they worry about the Fountain of Youth and what might happen if it goes wrong, and have half a dozen plans for how to deal with things if the O5s are corrupted, along with at least three lines of succession planned out to get the best possible replacements for them. And the Fountain of Youth is honestly less dangerous than this one is.

Even if the author disagrees with my water plan, it would be simple enough to have the O5s claim that there are back-up plans and leave it at that. But the idea that this guy is just so friendly that of course even the O5s trust him is ludicrous. I mean, imagine if this were a humanoid SCP. We'd be hearing shouts of "Mary Sue!!!" and no doubt "The Abel line!!!!!" until the page died horribly in a sea of downvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that part needs to be changed, yes. Exactly what to, I'm not sure offhand…there isn't much of a backup plan you can have in the sort of situation that would occur if things went downhill, except make sure you stay on good terms with 1073-2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I downvoted this the instant I saw an O5 saying "Don't worry about it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that the sapient water SCP that I read a few days ago?

If so, you really did pull it through. Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
by any chance, have you read any Xanth novels?

(upvoted!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the grammar help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know much m'self, but it seems to me the discussion you put in the containment procedures would better fit after the description. And I took the liberty of fixing two or three grammar issues.
